groups.explanation: The harness correctly passes fuzzer-controlled bytes to H5LTopen_file_image, which is the intended API for parsing in-memory HDF5 images. The OOM occurs because H5C__load_entry (H5Centry.c:1155) calls H5MM_realloc with actual_len (~4 GB) derived from a size field in the file data. The EOA guard (H5C__verify_len_eoa) is ineffective because EOA itself is populated from the fuzzer-controlled superblock (H5Fsuper.c:606: sblock->driver_addr + HDR_SIZE sets EOA), so an attacker-crafted driver_addr makes EOA arbitrarily large, allowing actual_len to pass the check and trigger the enormous allocation. The library must not trust in-file size/address fields for allocations without bounding them against the physical size of the image buffer.
groups.suggestedFixDirection: In H5C__load_entry (H5Centry.c around line 1147), before calling H5MM_realloc, add a check that actual_len does not exceed the physical size of the backing store (the in-memory image buffer size for core/image files), rather than relying solely on the EOA value that is itself derived from untrusted file data.
